title: NeuroCamp Practice
NeuroCamp Practice
section: Challenge 2 — Did the groups start out the same?
text:
Challenge 2 — Did the groups start out the same?
A fair experiment needs the groups comparable before treatment. Run a t-test on the before values across the two groups. Fill in the column being tested.












You’re checking the starting values, so the column is before.



t.test(before ~ trmt, data = sheep)
t.test(before ~ trmt, data = sheep)

title: NeuroCamp Practice
text:
Challenge 3 — Compute the p-value yourself
The shuffle gave us 1000 “luck” differences in luck, and our real difference is in real_diff. The p-value is the fraction of luck differences at least as big as ours. Fill in the column name.












Both luck and real_diff store the number in a column called stat.



mean(luck$stat >= real_diff$stat)
mean(luck$stat >= real_diff$stat)

title: NeuroCamp Practice
section: Challenge 4 — Does more data change the p-value?
text:
Challenge 4 — Does more data change the p-value?
We stack the data 3 times into big (same averages, same spread, just more rows). Run the t-test on big and see what the p-value does. Fill in which data frame to use.












Use the tripled data frame, big. Compare its p-value to the original.



t.test(change ~ trmt, data = big)
t.test(change ~ trmt, data = big)

title: NeuroCamp Practice
section: Challenge 5 — Be a skeptic: use the median

title: Is It Real, or Just Luck?
text:
For students: Run a code cell by clicking the Run Code button under it. You can change the numbers and run it again. You can’t break anything, so try stuff!
The very first cell takes a little while (your browser is downloading R). After that it’s fast.


1. Our question
This is data from a real experiment. Researchers gave 18 sheep heart attacks, then treated half with embryonic stem cells (esc) and left the other half untreated (ctrl). They measured each heart’s pumping capacity (the percent of blood the heart pushes out per beat) before and after.
Can stem cells help a damaged heart recover?
Let’s look at the data as a table, like a spreadsheet:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Each row is one sheep. The columns are its treatment group and its heart pumping capacity before and after. That’s all a dataset is: rows of subjects, columns of what we measured.
Notice the numbers aren’t all the same. That variation is what this whole lesson is about: where does it come from, and can the treatment explain some of it?






NoteThe same five steps, in any tool



Almost every data project runs the same loop, whether you use R, Python, or a spreadsheet:

Get the data in (load / import)
Clean it (fix labels, drop bad values)
Restructure it (make new columns, or reshape / pivot between “wide” and “long”)
Summarize it (averages, counts, by group)
Analyze it (charts, tests)

We’re using R today, but the thinking transfers straight to Excel or Google Sheets. Where it helps, we’ll show the spreadsheet version of a step too.



Why use code todayWhere a spreadsheet is great



It remembers every step, so anyone can re-run and check your work (reproducible).
It makes the shuffle simulation in section 5 easy. A spreadsheet really can’t.
With webr it runs in any browser, with nothing to install.




A quick look at small data, or a fast chart.
You may already know it, and the concepts are identical (clean, new columns, pivot, chart).
Totally fine for simple averages and graphs when you don’t need to reproduce or simulate.






2. Real data is messy, so clean it first
Real experiments never hand you tidy data. Here’s a messier version of the same study. Load it and see if you can spot the problems:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Three things are wrong. Can you find them?

A blank value (missing data).
An impossible value: an after of 425. Pumping capacity is a percent, so it can’t go above 100. Someone fumbled a decimal (42.5 became 425).
The labels don’t match: Ctrl, ctrl, esc, ESC, Esc. To the computer those are all different groups.

Let’s clean it:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Why this matters: one value of 425 instead of 42.5 would wreck every average and chart that follows. Cleaning is not busywork; it’s part of doing the science right.






TipSame idea in a spreadsheet



The exact same operations: Find & Replace to fix ESC/Esc into esc, then filter or sort to spot the blank and the 425 and delete those rows. Same moves, different buttons. This “clean before you analyze” step happens in every tool.




3. Build the number we actually care about
The question is about recovery, so what matters is how much each heart changed:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






TipSame idea in a spreadsheet



You’d add a new column with =after - before and fill it down. Building a calculated column is one of the most common data moves there is, in any tool. Its cousin is reshaping or pivoting, switching data between “wide” and “long”. Our before/after columns are “wide”; you’d pivot them “long” if you wanted to chart the change over time.


Now summarize each group: the typical change (average) and how much the sheep vary (spread).






TipPredict first



Before you run it: which group do you think improved more, and roughly by how much? Write down a number, then check.



Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The average is our best single guess for a group: if you had to predict one sheep’s change knowing only its group, you’d guess that group’s average. The spread is the leftover the average misses, how much real sheep bounce around that guess.
The stem-cell group’s average change is positive (hearts improved), the control group’s is negative (hearts got worse). But both groups have spread, so individuals overlap. Hold that thought.






TipSame idea in a spreadsheet



This grouped summary is exactly what a PivotTable does: drag treatment to the rows, drag change to the values, set it to “Average.” group_by() + summarise() is the PivotTable of the code world.




4. Picture it: choosing and making a graph
Always look at your data before trusting a single number.
Which graph? Match the chart to the question:

comparing groups of numbers → dot plot or box plot
one number over time → line chart
two numbers against each other → scatter plot







TipPredict first



Picture the plot before it appears: where do the stem-cell dots sit compared to the control dots, above or below the line at 0? Do they overlap?


Here every dot is one sheep’s change:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Add a box to show the middle 50% of each group:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Talk about it: the stem-cell group improved more, but the groups overlap. Some control sheep did better than some treated sheep.


5. Is it real, or just luck?
The stem-cell group changed more on average. But does that prove stem cells work?
If we split these 18 sheep into two random groups by coin flip, the two averages still wouldn’t be exactly equal. Random groups differ a little, by luck alone. So we need to tell a real difference from a lucky one.
The trick: if the treatment did nothing, the labels esc and ctrl would be meaningless, and we could shuffle them and get a difference just as big. So let’s shuffle 1000 times and see what luck produces.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.






TipPredict first



Make the call now, before the computer shows you: is this difference real, or could luck alone explain it? Commit to an answer.



Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Read the picture: if the red line is way out past the pile of “luck” differences, then luck almost never makes a gap this big, so the difference is probably real. If the red line sits inside the pile, luck makes gaps like this all the time, and we can’t claim stem cells helped.






ImportantWhy a coding tool instead of a spreadsheet?



You could do most of the earlier steps in Excel. But this one, shuffling the labels 1000 times and rebuilding the picture, is painful by hand and nearly impossible to do cleanly with clicks. Code also remembers every step, so anyone can re-run and check your work. That reproducibility, plus simulations like this, is the real reason scientists reach for code.


That shaded area has a name: the p-value. The shortcut scientists use instead of shuffling is the t-test:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Find the p-value in the output.

Small p-value (less than 0.05): hard to explain by luck → probably real.
Big p-value: luck could easily do this → we can’t claim a real difference.







ImportantTwo honest warnings (real scientists get these wrong!)




A small p-value does not mean the effect is “big” or “important.” Only “probably not luck.” The size of the change (a few percent) is a separate question.
A p-value is not “the chance I’m wrong.” It’s “how often pure luck would fool me.”





6. Your turn






NoteWait, where is R running?



There’s nothing to install and no cloud account. This page is R: a tool called webr runs R right inside your browser tab. Bookmark this link and it still works later. One catch: if you refresh the page it resets, so copy out any code or results you want to keep.


Same logic works for your NeuroCamp experiments. Did your habituated C. elegans respond differently from the controls? Did the treated earthworms differ? Whenever you compare two groups, the recipe is the same:

Get the data in and clean it.
Build the measure you care about, then summarize it (average + spread).
Plot it (dots + box). Do the groups overlap a lot, or barely?
Test it: is the difference real, or just luck?

Try it with your own numbers. Type your two groups into the cell below (replace the example, keep the quotes and commas), then run it:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Now run the same moves on your data, one cell at a time:

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.

Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
Got a lot of data, or want to keep your work? Two easy options: come back to this page and re-type it later, or do the same steps in a spreadsheet (clean, calculated column, PivotTable, chart, T.TEST) using the “Same idea in a spreadsheet” notes above.


7. Going further (finished early? want a harder problem?)
These go past the basics. Try each one before opening the solution. The earlier cells (sheep, real_diff, luck) are still loaded, so you can build on them.

Challenge 1 — How big is the effect, really?
A small p-value says “probably real,” not “big.” Find the actual size of the difference, then divide it by the typical spread to get an effect size. Is this a big effect, or a tiny one that we only noticed because the data was clean?






NoteSolution






Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The difference is about 8 percentage points, and the effect size is close to 2, which means the gap is almost two whole spreads wide. That’s a large effect, not just a barely-detectable one. Always report the size, not only the p-value.





Challenge 2 — Did the two groups start out the same?
A fair experiment needs the groups to be similar before treatment, otherwise one had a head start. Check whether the before values were comparable across groups. Why does this matter for trusting the result?






NoteSolution






Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The baseline averages are close and the p-value is large, so there’s no real difference at the start. Random assignment did its job. That’s what lets us credit the after difference to the stem cells rather than to a head start.





Challenge 3 — Compute the p-value yourself
The shaded area in section 5 is the p-value. Instead of reading it off the plot, count it: what fraction of the 1000 shuffles produced a difference at least as big as the real one?






NoteSolution






Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
That proportion is the p-value, built from scratch. It should land close to the t-test’s p-value. The p-value was never magic, it’s just “how often luck matched or beat us.”





Challenge 4 — Does more data change the story?
Pretend we ran the same study with three times as many sheep by stacking the data on itself. The averages and spread stay identical. Predict what happens to the p-value, then check.






NoteSolution






Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
The p-value gets much smaller, even though the difference and the spread didn’t change at all. p-values depend on sample size: with enough data, even a tiny difference becomes “statistically significant.” This is exactly why Challenge 1 (effect size) matters.





Challenge 5 — Be a skeptic: is it driven by one weird sheep?
The mean is sensitive to outliers. Redo the shuffle test using the median difference instead of the mean. If the result still holds, it isn’t being carried by one unusual sheep.






NoteSolution






Please enable JavaScript to experience the dynamic code cell content on this page.
If this p-value is also small, the conclusion is robust: it survives switching from the mean to the median, so it’s not an artifact of one extreme value. Checking whether a result holds up under a different choice is real scientific thinking.




Real data: a randomized stem-cell trial in sheep (Menasché et al., via the openintro R package). Questions about your data? The DataSquad and the UCLA Data Science Center help students with exactly this.
